speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. I'm conducting an interview with Mojsze Ganc. Mojsze, can you tell me what life was like for you in Poland in the 1920s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very good. I was always a little grown up boy and I was working, but life was very, very bad. Very bad conditions. That's what-- that's what [INAUDIBLE]. Very bad conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were the conditions bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't have enough money to get-- to live like humans. Everything was [? short ?] like humans. To keep our family with five people-- five children-- was a very hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you suffer any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special, but we were feeling very, very anti-Semitism. It was feeling. The question is because we didn't have any business. Business people, they was feeling the anti-Semitism. We was only ordinary people, working class people. We didn't come into collect-- in touch with-- with-- with the Polish people who think like that. We didn't feel that much. We feel. You [? not a ?] [INAUDIBLE]. [? or you did. ?] But I mean, people went about their business, what-- [? what ?] they feel very much about [? that. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there restrictions on your life because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special, but Christian-- but we feel we was-- we was-- was-- was like second [? right ?] citizens, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact at that time with Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean Gentile?

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Didn't have any-- not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect did Hitler's rise to power have on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only on my life, but on all Yiddish life. Because we know-- we knew what is the-- what-- we knew right from the-- from '33 since he took over, we knew what he's go-- what he's doing, what he's going to do with our Yiddish people. We knew already. [INAUDIBLE] book, his dirty murder book we knew already what he was going to do. But we didn't expect it to be like that, to be able to kill six million Jewish people. We didn't expect it, how it can happen in the 20th century to be people like that to kill them. But we know that he is not a friend of Jewish, he's an enemy of Jew. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what effect did he have on your family, friends, and the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very not good. Not good. Not good. Because we know what is-- I tell you before-- we knew about what is-- what is-- what is the future what he wanted to do, what he according to his book, his dirty book, we knew already what he's going to do. But we didn't expect it. We didn't expect it to-- to be like that with the concentration camps, with the gas chambers. We didn't expect it. How I can expect it? And a matter of fact, I'll tell you the Yiddish people used to say like that, Germany was a cultured country and it has three or four newspapers daily. How it can be expected in Poland? Who can afford the paper? Two-- two or three families used to buy a paper because they can't afford them. But over there, in [INAUDIBLE] was three, four newspapers. Cultured people. And how you can expect it from them to be [? helped ?] like that, to be killed. What condition got killed, that's what [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any members of your family want to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you what, not me family. Any Jewish person, if you wake him up in the middle from the night, you ask him, you want to go to Australia, to Argentina, to America, to England, he would-- he would left everything, he would go. But they didn't have a chance. You must have a permit, you must have money, you must have this, you must have [? relation. ?] But you-- but-- when they ask you to come. But otherwise, any Jewish person if you would ask him, you go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Because [? you won't ?] get of the anti-Semitic Polish-- Polish government. How anti-Semitic were the Polish government--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean are? You can't measure with a tape measure. You can measure it [INAUDIBLE] they are [INAUDIBLE] the both. They're both anti-Semitic, that's all. They try to-- to boycott Yiddish business, they try to-- not to deal with Yiddish people. And that's how-- what that-- you can't measure it on a tape measure. You can't measure it on a scale. High [INAUDIBLE] both. You can't measure. Anti-Semitic but that's all. [? They got ?] anti-Semitic fascists, that's all. That's enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what you recall about the day war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a story-- a very terrific story. Anyway, I must give up. In 1939, in first of September, Friday-- I remember like it was today-- I went to work. I was working. It's a beautiful day-- Friday, a beautiful day, a sunny day. I come to work, I start to work. 8:00, that's the time I started work. I did work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ten past nine I remember like it was [INAUDIBLE]. My boss-- my [INAUDIBLE], they called [? maester, ?] boss-- he thought he would put on Berlin on the radio and he would listen what Berlin say. A radio in that time, who can afford to have a radio? Transistor didn't exist. Only [? single ?] people in the [INAUDIBLE] bought radios. I remember there was a man who used to have an electric shop. He used to live in the Main Street in [INAUDIBLE]-- the Main Street in [INAUDIBLE]. He used to live on the third floor on a balcony. He would come home from the shop, he used to take the radio when it was a nice day, he put the radio on the balcony. He put the radio on. We young people like I was, we're standing on the ground on the footpath and listen to the radio to the news, the music. We enjoyed, but who ever buy a radio? But my boss have a radio. Because he was well-off, he bought a radio.

speaker: interviewee
text: A radio was [? a sort of ?] big box like this television. And from this radio, you can hear from all over the world-- capital cities all over the world. He just pressed the button, it [INAUDIBLE], he put on Berlin. Here was Berlin's [? sign. ?] Hitler speaking, from nine o'clock in the morning till twelve o'clock in the afternoon. He was speaking non-stop. I don't think [INAUDIBLE] I pick up the glass [INAUDIBLE]. Because he never stopped for a second. And what he was talking, us German language and Yiddish language, some words are similar. I understand what he was saying. He was saying, the bloody Jewish, they are everywhere. They are the banks, they are the business, they are the government. They make for trouble for all over the world. We will get rid of them, that's what we understand a few words. Seven o'clock, he finished-- I say he finished barking. I call-- say barking [INAUDIBLE] something like that. I say he finished barking. Seven o'clock, my boss say, Mojsze, they will put on [? there ?] Warsaw, capital city for Poland. After they put on Warsaw, we hear already different sort of things. And this time when Hitler was speaking from nine o'clock in the morning till twelve, and this time the German Army crossed the border without any-- without any-- declaring war. And they already occupied this town, mentioned the [INAUDIBLE]. And they mentioned [INAUDIBLE] how many people got killed, how many-- how many buildings was destroyed. Everything, every detail I was mentioned. Oh, war without declaring, without saying. That's what he done. And if he wanted to take a town that was far from the border, with a [? descent, ?] he dropped. You know what the [? descent ?] mean. He put-- he dropped the army [? with the descent, ?] with airplanes. And when I was coming home-- I finished work Friday afternoon-- I finished work, I come home, Chelm was bombed. Chelm [INAUDIBLE] [? Street ?] was bombed, destroyed. Three people got killed already. And on Saturday-- on Sunday, we hear on the radio what the Germans doing to the Yiddish people in these places where it occupied. The best Yiddish peoples-- lawyers, doctors, solicitors, actors, writers, the rabbis-- they have to clean the toilet, the public toilets and sweeping-- sweeping the streets. They built special places to hang people. They took some people, the best people from the town-- from each town-- and they hang them in middle from the city, like in [? Elizabeth ?] or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they forced the-- the people from the town to go and have a look at the hanged people. And they tell the people, if you will be naughty, you will finish up like that. You mean [? what you mean. ?] If your will be against. That's what. How you like that? So how did your life change? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: What change? I never changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this day, I went to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after German start occupied from the west, Russian Army went, occupied Poland from the east. And they start occupying, occupying, occupying one town after the other. As the border was open because the Russian Army went into Poland, the border was open, thousands of Jewish people from the town, from the places Hitler had occupied, left and they went to Russia. Of course, the border was open. Without any questions, Russia let them go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. People went in. Then the Red Army already not far from Chelm. One day, a-- a-- a Russian tank-- a Russian tank fast went through [? Chelm ?] Street, the main street. A tank, a small tank compared with the tanks that we [INAUDIBLE], small just a little small sports car. But it's fast. These-- these people [INAUDIBLE] they knew already that's the spying, they're spying what Chelm look like. They was like, that's [INAUDIBLE] spying. A few days later, the Red Army in Chelm-- Red Army in Chelm. Compare the atrocities what the German done to the Yiddish people in the places [INAUDIBLE] and compare with the Russian Army that was coming in, was a heaven. The only thing both happened, the anti-Semite-- the Polish anti-Semite from Chelm, they hiding because they're frightened. They're hiding like they can't see it. And then after that, after a few days, was a agreement with [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], the two foreign ministers, [INAUDIBLE], the German foreign minister, both of the Russian foreign minister where the new border will be. And I listen-- I sit back at work and I listen to the radio are I hear [INAUDIBLE] mention where the new border will be. They mentioned every village, every-- every little town, every town where the new border will be. [? As ?] [? we ?] [? looked ?] [? it ?] Chelm will be under the German. Oh. Not good. As I was-- and they-- and they keep repeating the news-- they keep repeating this news-- they keep repeating maybe 10, 12 times. As I was sitting and listening to the news, there-- the new Russian-- the new Russian and German border will be-- that mean Chelm will be in the [INAUDIBLE]. If the Russian Army went too far, they will go back. If the Germany Army went too far, they will go back. That mean-- the border is border.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Chelm will be under the German. As I was sitting and working, I thought myself, should I wait here till the German will come and they kill me? No. If you get a chance to go to Russia without any trouble, why you have to stay by the German to get killed? I was sitting in work after they finished-- like they keep repeating the news, special repeating about 10, 12 times. After they finished the news, I think myself, all right. I told my boss, [INAUDIBLE] Goldfarb, Mr. Goldfarb, tonight I finish work, tomorrow six o'clock in the morning, I go to Russia. As he hear what I'm saying, he tell me, you're a bloody beggar. That's what he said to me. What, you are frightened Hitler will take away your life? That's what he said. Then he repeat the same thing. I think myself, I never hear from this man some words like [INAUDIBLE] to speak to me. This the man [INAUDIBLE] pick me up for-- to be his apprentice. He pay me $0.10 [INAUDIBLE]. How he speak to me like that? Because he have too much work and he didn't have who to finish the work. I'll tell him, Mr. Goldfarb, you know me, my character. If I say something, I do it because I don't want to get killed by the bloody German. I want to be alive. Please pay me the money which you owe me and tonight I finish and tomorrow morning I'm going to Russia. From our town, Chelm, to the new Russian border, was only about 10 or 12 kilometers. For me, for a young man to walk 10 or 12 kilometers is like nothing. Anyway, and he still keep talking, talking, [INAUDIBLE] me not to go, not to go, wouldn't help you nothing. I am going, that's all. In the afternoon, I finish work, he pay me-- [INAUDIBLE]. Now let me speak. How much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a very interesting thing-- interesting thing. Go on. Go on. No. I will not say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did he pay you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What he pay me wasn't bad. But the [INAUDIBLE] month [INAUDIBLE] he pay me $0.10 a week, [INAUDIBLE] something like that. That type of money, this was money. But after that, when I improving in my working, he pay me more. But [INAUDIBLE]. I want increase every couple months, something like that-- increase in the wages, that's all. Of course, I got increases in my work, I got more-- more-- new-- the job to do it [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you returned home on that Friday evening? What did you say to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: What Friday evening? Oh, Friday evening. [SIGH] That was not Friday evening, that was a-- that's a couple of days later because the Friday evening, that was the first day when the-- when the German start to cross the border and when they bombed Chelm. But that was nothing, that was-- but what will be-- [INAUDIBLE]. But after-- on Saturday, on Sunday-- we hear what the German is doing to the Jewish people. The hang him and shoot him, and they kill him. That's all. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided yet nothing till the Russian-- till the Red Army crossed the border. Then I decided because before I didn't-- I can't say nothing. I haven't got anywhere to go because the Russian border is closed, you can't go to Russia. But when the Russian Army crossed the border, they opened the border and everybody can go. And I will. Let me [? speak lighter. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me where you went and who knew went with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- anyway, and this story when I left work-- I must tell you this story. When I left work this day, the boss didn't pay me. And a tell him goodbye and I hope I don't see you again. I didn't see him again because he was-- for sure, he was one of the six million, for sure he was. I look on the street where I used to work, [? Pialska ?] Street. Nobody on the street. All the people died. I don't know where the people gone-- nobody. When I come to the corner, [? Pialska ?] Street and [? Leblina ?] Street-- that's the [? my street, ?] [? Liblina ?] Street, like here Elizabeth or something like that-- in this corner used to be a Yiddish restaurant. And the name from the man from the restaurant, his name is [? London, ?] but we used to call-- the Yiddish people used to call him God because he was a very religious man. I look on him, he's already not daring the Yiddish clothes what I got here, the Yiddish clothes-- the special hat, the black suit, black coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: He [INAUDIBLE] European clothes. And I talk and I said, what are you [INAUDIBLE] to talk [INAUDIBLE] go [INAUDIBLE] and see what I-- I have do it. I look across the road was a big Catholic church. This few Polish fascists, while I was hiding, they already [INAUDIBLE] because they [INAUDIBLE] already, the Germans will come to [? meet ?] [? them. ?] You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? find ?] [? God, ?] I come not far from my place where I used to live was a place they used to call the [? bazaar. ?] You know [? bazaar? ?] [? Bazaar. ?] In this place, we used to meet each other, Polish people-- Yiddish people. If I want to meet you on the [? bazaar, ?] you be there on the [? bazaar. ?] I come there, my girlfriend is there standing and talking with other-- two boys, with a girl talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask, Mojsze, did you hear the news? Of course I hear the news. My boss had a radio, I heard the news. What are you going to do it? I'm going home, pack-- take a pair of trousers, a shirt, a pair of shoes and this and that-- and tomorrow six o'clock, I'm going to Russia. And I ask you to do the same thing if you want to be alive; otherwise, you will be finished. Hitler will not let you alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: One boy said, I just started to do a partnership with another man in a business. I cannot leave this one. And the girl, we known each other because we were friends, they girl was [INAUDIBLE] I know-- my friends, we knew each other. I already make arrangement to get married. I cannot go. All right. Do what you like it. I going. Tomorrow morning, six o'clock in the morning, I'm going to Russia. I took my girlfriend home-- I took her home. She was living on this corner of this street where when you are leaving Chelm, you must go through this-- through here, where she was living. I tell her, [INAUDIBLE], stay here tomorrow, six o'clock in the morning. I will wait for you till 7:00. I will not go [INAUDIBLE] you because according to Jewish law, according to Jewish way of life, it not for a girl to go away with a boy without marriage. That's not the way. According to the Jewish-- not just our religion, but a way of life. I will come to your home, call you, stay here till seven o'clock. I give her the option. If you will be here at seven o'clock, I will go. I will be in the little town [INAUDIBLE], a little town, I will be there. I will stay on the road where if you come to this little town, you must come through this road. I will stay there three days waiting for you. And I tell her, look here, [INAUDIBLE] we already know each other for four years. Nearly every day in the year, we meet each other. I don't want to separate from you. Be there at six o'clock. If not, be there three days. I as there six o'clock in the morning, she's not there. I wait till seven o'clock, she's not there. I went. Two boys, my friends, went together. As you come to the border, 12 kilometers, something like that, 15 kilometers-- I don't remember how much-- we see few thousand people-- Yiddish people-- fighting. One Russian soldier was coming. He let us go through the bridge, a little bridge. He let us go through to Russia. He didn't ask if we are communist, if we are against communist, if we've got passport, if we've got visa. We went through. First night, I was sleeping in a shed just across the border. Next morning, I went to that little town [INAUDIBLE] waiting for [INAUDIBLE] to come. I was waiting two days or one day or one and a half days, I can't remember. She was coming. She was coming. [? We was already ?] together. All right. In this little town, a little town, the population-- the Yiddish population was tripled, four times that much what it was before because all the people from the occupied places from Germany was coming to escape to Russia and they was there. And then you have in any place we was going in, please let me have a sleep on the ground, on the floor. Let me have a sleep. No room, no room.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only place we can be able to sleep is the synagogue. Also in the synagogue, if you come to synagogue four o'clock, five o'clock, it's too late-- already full. You had [INAUDIBLE] to sit there-- to lie there-- to sit there on the floor. No room. We were sleeping in the park. Can you believe it? We were sleeping in the park because it was summer. I find a little piece of tin, a little piece of tin like that-- dirty, rusty. I clean it, wash it and bent the tin like a plate. I used to go to the Red Army barracks and they used to give me a bit of kashi. That was the meal that I eat, like rice. They used to give me a bit of kashi and a bit-- and a bit of bread and sometimes a bit of meat, a piece of meat. This was what our food, that's what I eat. What we should do? What we should do? What [? you ?] be? A lot of people decided to go back, go back to the Germans, go back to Poland where they come from. I said, I am not going back. I am not going back. I will be-- I don't want to get killed by the bloody Germans. Something will come on. I must tell you, when my mother with my grandmother was talking about me to become apprentice, my grandmother was saying, God will help. You see, God will help. He will become apprentice, God will help.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you are religious or not religious, but still decide you were brought up in this-- this-- this-- this life, I thought to myself, maybe God will help. Maybe something-- a miracle will happen if something will happen, [? I'll ?] [? change. ?] And we hear that in Brest-Litovsk, a town not far from there, the Russian government, two [? offices ?] [? paid ?] [? the ?] [? Yiddish ?] people to go to Russia to work, volunteer. I went there. We took our parcels, we went there to the station, we went to Brest off from the train straight into the office. Was two offices. One office was to register people with families, with children, and the other office was to register single people. Can be married, but not children. We register, we get half a bread. I got half a bread, [INAUDIBLE] got half a bread, a herring-- a salted herring, a big herring, and a bit of sugar, and a number for the carriage. We have to go to the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see about 25 carriages, different sized carriages. And each character [INAUDIBLE] according the carriage can take-- 20 people, 10 people, 15 people. And we started-- after two days, all the carriages was full, we start to move. We moves like a fly [INAUDIBLE], so slowly, slowly, slowly till we come to the old Russian-Polish border. What you think about my story? We'll finish the tape two here and we'll continue on tape three. Thank you.
